conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. I'm looking to find out if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. 

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to ask some questions about their individual circumstances.

Let me start with some basic information: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 7 years old, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Based on the ages, Alex and Charlie (both 7) would generally fall within the age criteria, but Blake at 20 would typically be outside the usual age range unless they're in approved education or training.

**Could you tell me if Blake is currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training. He actually works full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying about Blake.

Now I need to understand the living arrangements for your children. **Do all three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie - currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me, and I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to check a few more details.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Just to confirm one more detail about Blake: **How many hours per week does Blake work in his full-time job?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He works full-time - it's over 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: **Are any of your children currently in hospital, in care, or receiving certain benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are in hospital or care, and they don't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on the eligibility rules for Child Benefit, here's my assessment:

**You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for:**
- ✅ **Alex** - As they are 7 years old (under 16), live with you in the UK, and meet all the eligibility criteria.
- ✅ **Charlie** - As they are also 7 years old (under 16), live with you in the UK, and meet all the eligibility criteria.

**You are NOT eligible for Child Benefit for:**
- ❌ **Blake** - Although Blake is under 20, they are not in approved education or training and work full-time (over 24 hours per week). Under the rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie, but not for Blake.

Please note: If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is not in hospital, care, or receiving benefits in their own right. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old. The normal age limit for Child Benefit is under 16, or under 20 if the child is in approved education or training. Blake is not in approved education or training and works full-time (over 24 hours per week). According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 7 years old, which is under 16, so they fall within the age criteria for Child Benefit. Charlie lives with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie is not in hospital, care, or receiving benefits in their own right. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
